frameworks.name: divisive normalization
frameworks.one_line: Canonical neural gain-control rule applied to consumer valuation.

frameworks.name: discrete choice model
frameworks.one_line: Standard discrete choice framework augmented with a normalization stage.

frameworks.name: range normalization
frameworks.one_line: Alternative context-dependent valuation rule the authors test against.

frameworks.name: efficient coding under neurobiological constraints
frameworks.one_line: Motivates normalization as an error-minimizing representation.

core_question: Can divisive normalization of valuations explain how consumer choice depends on the size and composition of the choice set?
key_assumptions: Valuations are transformed during the choice process, not fixed a priori.
Neural representations face fixed capacity / neurobiological constraints.
Substitution patterns reveal the underlying valuation transform.
manipulations: Size of the choice set was varied across trials.
Composition of the choice set was varied across trials.
Two distinct choice-experiment paradigms were used.
key_findings: Choice behaviour depends jointly on choice-set size and composition.
Divisive normalization fits observed choices better than range normalization and other alternatives.
The result is robust across both experimental paradigms.
interpretations: Divisive normalization is an efficient valuation code that minimizes choice errors given neural constraints.
Context-dependent preferences emerge from a canonical neural computation rather than ad hoc choice heuristics.
